perturbation.original_text: 16. The prevailing party, or if there is not one, the substantially prevailing party, in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to attorneys fees and costs in addition to any other available relief.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall entitle a party to rely on the other or its advisors, or require a party to enter into any agreement or transaction, or preclude a party from entering into any agreement or transaction or pursuing its lines of business, or obligate either party to the other party in any other way, except as expressly provided in this Agreement or in any other written agreement existing or entered into by the parties hereafter. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall compel either party to furnish information to the other party or to negotiate any transaction. 16. The prevailing party, or if there is not one, the substantially prevailing party, in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to attorneys fees and costs in addition to any other available relief.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the attorney's fees clause into the section about 'no obligation' obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed, potentially violating principles of fair disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1717 addresses attorney's fees and states, that in any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney's fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees in addition to other costs. By placing this provision in a section about the lack of obligation, it could be argued that it is not being presented fairly and clearly, potentially undermining its enforceability under California law, which expects contracts to be interpreted in a way that gives effect to the mutual intention of the parties, as it existed at the time of contracting (California Civil Code § 1636).

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s f
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts legal principles by embedding the attorney's fees clause within a section that disclaims obligations, which undermines its prominence and enforceability. According to California Civil Code § 1717, as evidenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, attorney's fees provisions must be clearly stated and applied to the entire contract. By placing this provision in a section about the lack of obligation, it could be argued that it is not being presented fairly and clearly, potentially undermining its enforceability under California law, which expects contracts to be interpreted in a way that gives effect to the mutual intention of the parties, as it existed at the time of contracting.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of California, U.S.A. without reference to conflict of law principles. Any disputes under this Agreement may be brought in the state courts or the Federal courts located in Alameda County, and the parties hereby consent to the exclusive subject matter personal jurisdiction and venue of these courts. This Agreement may not be amended except in writing signed by both parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Upon the written request of the other party, each party shall promptly return to the other (or destroy if destruction is requested and provide written certification of destruction) all documents and other tangible and intangible materials representing the other’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof. 13. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of California, U.S.A. without reference to conflict of law principles. Any disputes under this Agreement may be brought in the state courts or the Federal courts located in Alameda County, and the parties hereby consent to the exclusive subject matter personal jurisdiction and venue of these courts. This Agreement may not be amended except in writing signed by both parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law and venue selection clause to the section about returning confidential information makes it less noticeable and harder to find when a dispute arises.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a) allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. While the contract specifies venue in Alameda County, placing this clause in the return of materials section could be seen as an attempt to obscure the venue selection and potentially undermine its enforceability, especially if the chosen venue is inconvenient or unjust for one of the parties. This challenges the principle that venue selection clauses should be clear and conspicuous.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces a structural flaw by moving the governing law and venue selection clause to a less conspicuous section about returning confidential information. This undermines the enforceability of the venue selection clause because it obscures its prominence. According to 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a), a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. By obscuring the original venue selection, the changed text makes it more difficult to enforce the agreement to venue, creating a contradiction. The original contract ensures clarity on venue, while the changed version attempts to hide it, impacting the fairness and enforceability of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 7. This Agreement shall continue in full force and effect for so long as the parties continue to exchange Confidential Information. This Agreement may be terminated by either party at any time upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party, however its terms shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Neither party shall knowingly communicate any information to the other in violation of the rights of any third party. 7. This Agreement shall continue in full force and effect for so long as the parties continue to exchange Confidential Information. This Agreement may be terminated by either party at any time upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party, however its terms shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the termination clause to the section about third-party rights makes it less obvious, impacting a party's understanding of their ability to end the agreement and the duration of confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1654 states that in cases of uncertainty in a contract, the language should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. By placing the termination clause in a seemingly unrelated section regarding third-party rights, it creates ambiguity and makes it harder for a party to understand their rights and obligations regarding termination. This obscurity could lead to disputes and potentially violate the principle that contracts should be interpreted in a clear and understandable manner, in favor of the party who did not cause the uncertainty.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1654. In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract moves the termination clause (clause 7) into a section (clause 14) concerning third-party rights. This relocation obscures the clause, making it less discoverable and impacting a party's understanding of their ability to terminate the agreement and the duration of confidentiality obligations. This obscurity violates California Civil Code § 1654, which states that in cases of uncertainty, the contract language should be interpreted against the party who caused the uncertainty. By creating ambiguity through the relocation of the clause, the changed contract violates the principle of clear understanding and potentially disadvantages a party. This falls under the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.
